acceptance_criteria.text: Multi-user authorization for EVE linking, character selection, thread ownership, and callback state is anchored to user_id on live paths. Legacy telegram_sessions.oauth_state is no longer accepted by the EVE callback, and user-scoped code paths no longer fall back to chat_id when a real user_id is available.
proof: src/auth/auth-request.ts stores live callback state only in auth_requests.
src/web/auth-routes.ts rejects missing or stale auth_requests state and no longer reads telegram_sessions.oauth_state on the live callback path.
src/eve/sso.ts resolves live user-scoped ownership from user_id and only uses chat_id in contexts that truly lack a resolved user.
src/db/migrations.ts clears retained telegram_sessions.oauth_state values.
tests/unit/auth-routes.test.ts and tests/unit/sso.test.ts cover the callback and user-owned character behavior.

acceptance_criteria.text: Telegram request handling has explicit in-process abuse controls for public multi-user use: one active agent request per chat, bounded recent request rate per Telegram user/chat, and a global active-request ceiling with user-facing overload errors plus regression coverage.
proof: src/telegram/request-guard.ts implements overlap rejection, recent-rate limiting, and a global active-request ceiling.
src/telegram/handlers.ts evaluates the guard before agent execution and returns retry-later messages.
src/config.ts exposes the guard thresholds as runtime config.
tests/unit/telegram-request-guard.test.ts covers overlap, global overload, rate window exhaustion, and window expiry.
gaps: The guard is in-memory and therefore scoped to the single-process deployment model.

acceptance_criteria.text: Telegram-to-web handoff no longer sends the one-time bearer token in a server-observed query string. The browser completes handoff through a fragment-driven flow and a POST exchange endpoint, with updated route coverage and docs.
proof: src/telegram/handlers.ts now emits /auth/tg-handoff#token=... links.
src/web/frontend.ts serves the handoff shell and client/src/app.tsx completes exchange from location.hash via POST /auth/tg-handoff/exchange.
src/web/auth-routes.ts implements POST /auth/tg-handoff/exchange with Cache-Control: no-store.
tests/unit/frontend-handoff.test.ts and tests/unit/auth-routes.test.ts cover the new handoff path.

acceptance_criteria.text: Model-facing profile context is hardened against prompt injection without stripping gameplay detail from USER.md. Generated USER.md keeps full in-game information, while prompt ingestion treats that content as untrusted data with explicit delimiting and prompt-safety handling.
proof: src/eve/user-profile.ts still renders rich gameplay data including wallet, fittings, clones, implants, attributes, and skill queue.
src/agent/prompts.ts wraps USER.md and summary content in explicit untrusted-data blocks and quotes each line.
tests/unit/user-profile.test.ts verifies the rich sections remain present and dangerous markup is sanitized.
tests/unit/prompts.test.ts verifies the prompt framing and quoted data lines.
gaps: Prompt footprint remains large by design because the user requested full USER.md content.

acceptance_criteria.text: Unlinking a character reduces retained sensitive state. When a character no longer has active links, encrypted token material and generated profile artifacts for that user/chat are removed, with regression coverage and updated security docs.
proof: src/eve/user-profile-storage.ts centralizes profile artifact deletion.
src/eve/sso.ts deletes the profile artifact on unlink and removes eve_accounts token material when a character becomes fully detached.
src/web/auth-routes.ts deletes stale profile artifacts during ownership reassignment.
tests/unit/sso.test.ts covers detached-character cleanup.

commands_for_fresh_verifier: npm test -- tests/unit/auth-routes.test.ts tests/unit/telegram-request-guard.test.ts tests/unit/frontend-handoff.test.ts tests/unit/prompts.test.ts tests/unit/sso.test.ts tests/unit/user-profile.test.ts
known_gaps: Telegram abuse controls are in-memory because the repository explicitly targets a single-process deployment model.
USER.md remains intentionally rich, so this task hardens prompt trust boundaries rather than reducing prompt footprint.
